subject: EU Law
lesson_title: Judicial Liability and National Procedural Autonomy

questions.question: The extension of state liability to judicial acts was established in which case?

options.D: Gerhard Köbler v Republik Österreich [2007] (on remand)

questions.explanation: Köbler v Austria (C-224/01, 2003) established that state liability extends to judicial acts, specifically judgments of courts of last instance. Professor Köbler claimed the Austrian Administrative Court had breached EU law by refusing to award him a loyalty bonus that he argued was due under free movement of workers law. The CJEU held that, in principle, a member state may be liable for damage caused by a decision of a court of last instance that infringes EU law.

questions.question: Why did the CJEU restrict judicial liability to courts of last instance in Köbler?

options.A: Because lower courts are not subject to EU law obligations
options.B: Because courts of last instance are the final guarantors of correct application of EU law in domestic proceedings — their errors are irreversible within the national system, making a damages remedy the only effective corrective
options.C: Because lower courts can always be appealed, so EU law is not definitively at risk until the court of last instance rules
options.D: Both B and C — the irreversibility of the decision and the opportunity for internal correction at lower levels

questions.explanation: The CJEU in Köbler limited liability to courts of last instance for two connected reasons: (1) Courts of last instance are the final domestic arbiters — their erroneous application of EU law cannot be corrected by appeal, making a damages claim the only available remedy; (2) Lower courts' errors can in principle be corrected within the appellate hierarchy. The principle of legal certainty (finality of judgments) also required the threshold to be particularly high for judicial liability.

questions.question: What is the threshold for judicial liability (established in Köbler and confirmed in Traghetti del Mediterraneo)?

options.A: Any error of EU law by a court of last instance
options.B: A manifest infringement of applicable EU law — which requires taking into account the specific nature of the judicial function and applying the Brasserie factors, with a particularly high threshold given the principle of judicial independence
options.C: An infringement found by the CJEU in a preliminary reference in the same case
options.D: Any breach of the duty to refer under Article 267(3) TFEU

questions.explanation: Köbler and Traghetti del Mediterraneo (C-173/03, 2006) confirmed that the threshold for judicial liability is a 'manifest infringement' of applicable EU law — it is deliberately high to respect judicial independence and the finality of judgments. The Brasserie factors apply, but with the added consideration of the specific nature of the judicial function. In practice, Köbler-type claims succeed only in exceptional cases where the judicial error is obvious and inexcusable, especially where the court failed to refer despite being a court of last instance.

questions.question: In Traghetti del Mediterraneo v Italy [2006], Italy had enacted legislation limiting state liability for judicial acts to cases of intentional fault or serious misconduct. The CJEU held:

options.A: That Italian law was acceptable — member states may define the conditions for judicial liability in their national law
options.B: That Italian law was incompatible with EU law — member states may not exclude liability for manifest infringement of EU law by courts of last instance or limit it to intentional fault, as this would make it practically impossible to obtain reparation
options.C: That Italian law was acceptable as long as it applied equally to purely domestic and EU law claims
options.D: That Italian legislation limiting judicial liability was permissible provided it exempted cases where the CJEU had given a preliminary ruling

questions.explanation: Traghetti del Mediterraneo confirmed that member states cannot enact legislation that excludes judicial liability for manifest infringement of EU law or limits it to intentional fault or serious misconduct. Such legislation would make it practically impossible to obtain reparation, violating the effectiveness principle. The CJEU also held that excluding liability for interpretation of rules of law or assessment of facts/evidence — activities inherent in any judgment — was also incompatible with EU law when it prevented reparation for manifest infringements.

questions.question: The principle of 'national procedural autonomy' in EU remedies law means that:

options.A: Member states have complete freedom to determine all procedural aspects of EU law claims
options.B: In the absence of EU harmonisation, member states may apply their own procedural rules to EU law claims, subject to the twin constraints of equivalence and effectiveness
options.C: Member states may require claimants to exhaust all national administrative remedies before bringing a state liability claim
options.D: National courts may apply their own limitation periods to EU law claims without any constraint

questions.explanation: National procedural autonomy (established in Rewe and Comet [1976]) means that, in the absence of specific EU procedural rules, member states apply their own procedural law to EU law claims. This is necessary because the EU has not fully harmonised national procedures. However, two constraints apply: equivalence — procedures for EU law claims must not be less favourable than those for comparable domestic claims; effectiveness — procedures must not make it practically impossible or excessively difficult to exercise EU law rights.

questions.question: The equivalence principle, as applied to state liability claims, requires:

options.A: All member states to have identical procedural rules for state liability claims
options.B: National procedural rules applicable to state liability claims under EU law to be no less favourable than those applicable to similar claims based on domestic law
options.C: State liability claimants to receive exactly the same compensation as they would have received under the correctly implemented Directive
options.D: National courts to award damages equivalent to those awarded by courts in other member states for similar breaches

questions.explanation: Equivalence requires that the conditions for bringing a state liability claim under EU law are no less favourable than those for bringing an analogous claim under purely domestic law. For example, if a member state's national law allows a three-year limitation period for governmental liability claims, it cannot impose a one-year period for EU law state liability claims. The comparison is with the closest domestic equivalent — the test is whether EU law claims are disadvantaged compared to domestic ones.

questions.question: The effectiveness principle, as applied to state liability procedural rules, was applied in Levez v TH Jennings (Case C-326/96) to what national rule?

options.A: A national rule requiring pre-action mediation in equal pay claims
options.B: A two-year limitation period on back-pay claims under the Equal Pay Act — which was disapplied because the employer's deliberate concealment meant the claimant could not reasonably have brought her claim within the period
options.C: A rule requiring all state liability claims to be brought in administrative courts
options.D: A national rule capping damages at three months' salary

questions.explanation: Levez concerned a UK two-year limitation period for equal pay back-pay claims under the Equal Pay Act. The CJEU held that where the limitation period had run because the employer deliberately misled the employee about her colleagues' salaries, applying the time limit was contrary to the effectiveness principle — it made the exercise of EU rights practically impossible. The case illustrates the effectiveness principle's role in preventing national procedural rules from being used to frustrate EU law rights.

questions.question: Can a member state require a claimant in a state liability case to exhaust all domestic remedies before bringing a damages claim?

options.A: Yes — exhaustion of domestic remedies is always a valid procedural prerequisite under EU law
options.B: Conditionally — a requirement to use an available domestic remedy to mitigate loss is permissible, but only if the remedy is effective and its pursuit does not make the damages claim practically impossible or excessively difficult
options.C: No — EU law prohibits any exhaustion of remedies requirement in state liability claims
options.D: Yes — but only if the domestic remedy would have provided full reparation

questions.explanation: National courts may require claimants to take reasonable steps to mitigate their loss, including using available domestic remedies. However, this condition must not make reparation impossible or excessively difficult. If the domestic remedy is ineffective or its pursuit is unduly burdensome, requiring it as a precondition for state liability would violate the effectiveness principle. The key is that mitigation steps must be reasonable — not that all conceivable avenues be pursued.

questions.question: In Courage Ltd v Crehan [2001] (Case C-453/99), the CJEU held that state liability principles extended to which context?

options.A: Liability of EU institutions for legislative acts
options.B: The right of a party to an anti-competitive agreement to claim damages from the other (private) party that breached EU competition law — establishing private enforcement of EU competition law
options.C: Judicial liability for infringement of the Charter of Fundamental Rights
options.D: State liability for judicial acts in competition law proceedings

questions.explanation: Courage v Crehan extended the logic of Francovich/Brasserie to private (horizontal) relations in competition law. Crehan was a party to a pub tie agreement that breached Article 101 TFEU (then Article 81 EC). The CJEU held that any individual must be able to claim damages for loss caused by an agreement prohibited by EU competition law — including a party to the agreement itself. This established the private right of action for damages for breach of EU competition law, later codified in Directive 2014/104.

questions.question: Which of the following national procedural rules was held to be incompatible with EU law effectiveness in Emmott v Minister for Social Welfare [1991]?

options.A: A rule requiring all claims to be brought in writing rather than orally
options.B: A rule limiting retrospective claims under an equal treatment Directive — the CJEU held member states could not rely on limitation periods against individuals when the Directive had not been properly transposed
options.C: A rule requiring social welfare claimants to appeal to a specialist tribunal before applying to the court
options.D: A cap on compensation in discrimination cases

questions.explanation: In Emmott v Minister for Social Welfare (C-208/90), the CJEU held that where a member state has not properly transposed a Directive, it cannot rely on national limitation periods to defeat a claim based on that Directive. Until proper transposition, the limitation period cannot begin to run. This was a particularly strong application of the effectiveness principle, though subsequent cases (like Steenhorst-Neerings and Johnson) limited its scope — Emmott was confined to its specific facts (where the state's failure to transpose prevented the claimant knowing of her rights).
